column221:
I am a marine biologist from Australia. I love going to the beach and I work at the beach where I now teach marine science.

I am greatly concerned by the inaccessibility of coastal areas to children I teach from The Bay Area. I teach a lot of Title 1 schools and a number of them have never been to the beach before or never thought they would get to touch marine life in the tidepools. It is a big motivator for the program I teach.

Transport to the beaches is a big challenge. For public transport, there is only one bus that I know of from when I was living on the Sonoma coast and Im not sure if many other people know about it. Highway One is also a very dangerous road. I would like to see guard rails all the way along the edge, especially north from Jenner. Cars go over all the time, people die on that road - I hear it from the community even when I cant find reports in the news. I know people who don't like to drive the coast for this reason alone. That extends to bus companies. I have had school programs cancel because they were unable to find a bus driver in their area who would drive on the coast.

I would like to see greater invasive species management and removal. The coast is highly disturbed in terms of plant communities. Cow farming is used as a management tool in state parks but elk would be better.

An abalone population I was aware of in Fort Ross State Historic Park also just got poached. California State Parks try their best, Im not sure what else can be done about it.

There is limited signage encouraging people to keep their distance from marine mammals too, especially near known haul outs. I have seen many people disturb seals and sea lions causing large groups to flush into the water.

I would also like to see the reintroduction of sea sunflower sea stars to eat purple sea urchins and help bring back kelp forests.

column194: Yes, it is caused by both human activities and natural forces
column221:
Coastal areas are a huge part of Northern California coast culture. We are more rural and isolated from the rest of the state which allows for less crowded beaches, but this may also lead to less funding for marine resource management. Many species here are understudied or underresearched because we don't have a large university or PhD-granting institution up here, and it is hard to find funding for research in general. I think about many tribal species that we don't have accurate data for like smelts and perches. 

Another concern with MPAS is the idea of access. What is the MPA creating access for? I know in the case of certain MPAS it can harm native harvesters particularly those that harvest from shore, but recreational and commercial fishermen on boats may still have access (although gas costs increase and may prevent trips)

column221: There is no space for me to show that I have a lifeguard and kayak guide rescue team providing community education funded by tourism in the form of wildlife adventures by kayak and partner with NOAA and our State Parks since 1987. Most of our income funds our staff wages and upkeep of our operating equipment and location. The owner (Me) lives off of less then $20,000 a year in order to keep the programs for school field research, scout and other youth activities viable. Originally I volunteered but I have to pay our lifeguards and staff and cover the overhead. I also drop our tour rates to provide community education guided tours at least once a week that are more affordable for families and local residents who also work in tourism.  I really need some funding because I may be about to lose my low income health care.

column221:
The contemporary experience of people of color in Orange County and Newport Beach is deeply intertwined with a history of colonization, segregation, and discriminatory practices.

The land now known as Newport Beach was originally inhabited by Native peoples, including the Tongva and Juaneño/Luiseño nations, for thousands of years. Their established communities and land management practices were eventually displaced by European exploration and subsequent Spanish, Mexican, and American colonization.

As a result, environmental inequity significantly restricts beach access for people of colour due to historical segregation (e.g., Jim Crow, redlining) and ongoing systemic barriers. GIS mapping reveals these disparities: communities of colour are often located further from clean beaches, disproportionately near polluting industries, and face inadequate public transit, high parking fees, and a general lack of investment in nearby coastal amenities. This results in reduced recreational opportunities, exacerbated health issues from pollution, loss of cultural connection to coastal areas, and an overall reinforcement of socioeconomic inequalities.

column221:
As a 5th Generation California Chinese American, the ocean has always been somewhere that we drove to it is not an area that I ever lived. It's that close to it.

We've always made annual treks to the beach as a family, and then when I was in high school, we would go more frequently with friends.

When I had my own family and my children were little, we would also drive out to the beach and go to the beach. Maybe when we were on vacation throughout California, children always enjoy the ocean. And it's a pleasant place to be.

I feel so fortunate to live in California. Where I think the people value the ocean and all it represents and all that it can be back to the native Americans that have been here for millennia, but also yeah, the current californians that live here and understand its value and its beauty.

column221: I am a retired professor and marine biologist living close to the ocean in El Segundo, CA. Every day, I ride my bike to the beach to catch a few waves or walk a few miles along the beach watching the waves and observing marine life.  The ocean plays a key role in my life!  I'm concerned that our federal government is becoming less stable in protecting our marine waters, depending on which political party is in power.

This area's coastal area's lack life guards and the waters are not safe for most swimmers.

column221:
We have regulatory mechanisms in California to improve our water quality.  These mechanisms are only effective if there is community as well as business understanding and buy-in, if timelines for implementation are honored, and if regulations are enforced.  I have witnessed dedicated, knowledgeable, and highly-trained staff leaving regulatory positions because of failures of California's agencies tasked with carrying out these public services to implement and enforce regulatory plans that are already in place. I hope we will find a way to do better.
Thank you for your work in conducting this survey and your efforts to care for our oceans and coastline.

I don't have access to the protected zones, however I have visited on one occasion close to UCSB, and I have to say my mind was blown, so beautiful, I think I saw a sea wolf? for the first time.

I regularly will hit Santa Monica for the cooling weather, and to hop in the water. I am concerned with the human sunblock that gets in the water. I think we are an overcrowded area, but for the most part I am surprised, or I can tolerate or I amused to the cleanliness of the sea. Santa Monica is a pretty good municipality in comparison to the surrounding, I’m trying to do more at sea like visit the whole coast, snorkel, sail, but the buses are not that quick available or affordable to me. I have no income mostly so I navigate in a strange way.


Feel free to contact me if you want more info

Reducing barriers to help people to get to & connect with the ocean is incredibly important. Historically marginalized and excluded groups from resource management decisions face the most barriers when visiting the ocean. Local management should help increase equitable access and create spaces for communities to engage decision making. 

Sea level rise will reduce the amount of beach habitat we have by the end of the century. I truly believe that connecting people to a place helps people want to understand and protect it for future generations.

Thank you for your work protecting the coast!

column221: I spend a lot of time photographing and observing wildlife along the California coast. Most of my work focuses on natural behavior and non-invasive documentation using telephoto lenses. Spending that much time in different coastal ecosystems has shown me how much human presence, wildfire runoff, and shifting water quality can shape local conditions. I believe marine resource management works best when it balances access and conservation - protecting sensitive habitats while still helping people understand and connect with them.

column221:
Since Covid, I’ve seen an increase in people from other places visiting the beaches I frequent. Many of the visitors are not respectful of the local residents nor the environment. 

California State government leaders are claiming they are solving an “affordable housing crisis” by creating 100s of new housing laws. These laws directly benefit developers and degrade our coastlines. Most all of the developments rent or sell at market rate. Not only does this push out longtime locals to find more affordable housing, but it also takes a huge toll on our local infrastructure and coastal resources. 

We need to stop development in and near designated Coastal Zones. More people, more problems!!!

column221: i am a marine biology student, and avid diver. i love the oceans of california and think that there needs to be stronger, and stricter regulations on their use, and the resources that we take from them. as someone who can only speak more on the scientific side, the effects of climate change are starting to present themselves, and it’s extremely scary. i think that more money and government funding should be allocated to institutions (universities), and also organizations like NOAA, and that there should be more education on the serious effects of climate change on the ocean, and more people should be aware of the detrimental global effect of it, since people don’t realize that it will effect them even though they might not spend as much time there.

column221:
I have concerns about increasing availability of currently pristine areas to the general public.  Sadly, too many do not honor these areas - leaving behind many kinds of trash.  I think pristine areas are pristine BECAUSE there is limited access and development.  The Southern CA coast has many public beaches.  For me, encouraging use and respect for these is a high priority and is far more important than opening/developing additional coastal spaces. Future generations should have the privilege and awe of experiencing nature untouched. 

(I grew up in Ventura County frequently visiting Ventura beaches and have lived in Santa Barbara for the past fifty five years. In younger years I body surfed and did a bit of scuba diving. With family and friends I have also walked all the beaches from Hollister Ranch to Emma Wood State Park. A bit of walking effort can take one to many beautiful and uncrowded areas!)

column221:
The MPA system unfairly takes away from shore based fishermen in an effort to stop the negative impacts of commercial fishing operations (including sport boats) and spearfishing.

There is no scientific evidence to show that people fishing with hook and line from shore have any serious impact on the population of any species of fish or marine life. The maximum casting distance for your average fisherman is under 300ft. Someone really needs to think about how little sense it makes to primarily punish the shore based hook and line fishermen with the MPA system. Most subsistence fishing is done with hook and line.

We have the history of spearfishing giant sea bass at their spawning grounds almost to extinction and commercial fishing operations that still pick entire areas clean but the MPAs mostly take away unique fishing opportunities from shore based fishermen while letting the real offenders just move to a different area of the open ocean.

The very first MPA site of Laguna Beach cares more about using the MPA system as marketing for increasing their property values than they do about any kind of actual conservation or science based marine resource management. 

I feel fairly certain that I will never even be able to fish at places I spent many days of my childhood fishing with friends and forming lifelong memories. Those memories are all tainted now by this misguided program being pushed mainly by people who really do not care about or interact with the ocean at all. 

The less influence local people who do not interact with the ocean have over regulations the better. My experience is that they will just use it as an opportunity for performative activism on subjects they know nothing about.

column221: The  ‘Atáaxum, First People of this Land, are often excluded from coastal ecological discourse. We are a keystone species to the balance of the coasts’ ecosystems. Our absence is felt by the Land-especially in coastal conservation efforts. As a Native person I have witnessed large NGOs, State Parks, Coastal City Councils, and other organizations ignore comments, change the subject and avoid questions by California Natives in public meetings. Often they want to get their grant to pay themselves and their Non-Native employees and behave in ways that indicate they just want Tribal Peoples’ to be silenced. If conservationists really want to make change they should move into ways to give LAND BACK. When Native people return to stewardship we prioritize health of an ecosystem over profit. We know the land. We know how to care for it. When we return to stewardship -we protect all life.

column221:
I live here and choose to raise a family because of the ocean and the people. I love San Diego at my core. I learned to surf in PB and have kept that passion alive in OB/Sunset Cliff for a majority of the time I've lived here. I consider myself an OBecian at heart. 
I came to San Diego to work at Sea World, a job I truly loved. I strongly believe the Coastal Commission no longer has the interests of Californians at heart, but instead have created their own mafia style of governance; for which, they are not accountable to anyone. They are not protecting California's coasts, they are pandering to loud, minority voices that do not subscribe to facts. 
The Coastal Commission had no right to take away Sea World's captive breeding program. The education and oceanic appreciation Sea World provides and promotes is FAR greater than ANYTHING the Coastal Commission has ever accomplished! Plus, Sea World uses sales to fund animal rescues, the ONLY privately funded rescues in America.

column221:
Dear Management:

The power of the ocean and its mysteries are central to my spiritual and physical well being.  Living close to the ocean is imperative to my quality of life, and, in my opinion, the ocean and Golden Gate Park massively contribute to keeping San Francisco relatively sane.  They are our natural treasures for which we must garner and maintain compassionate stewardship and protect with every fiber of our being for, at least, the next seven generations to enjoy.  It is our human responsibility.  (FYI, I am, as we speak, watching the wonderful Netflix documentary OCEANS, narrated by Barack Obama.). 

Thank you for ALL that you do!!!!

Blessings, Giggles and Much Joy,
fannielee lowe

column221: I have lived in San Diego nearly my entire life and the ocean is a part of who I am. It wasn't until attending school in Santa Cruz and working within the Santa Cruz Climate Action program that I noticed that these spaces that I hold so near and dear to my heart are at risk. I also feel like these risks aren't talked about with the general public enough or they just focus on protecting the multi-million dollar homes that are built on a cliff edge. I am so proud that California does not have any private beaches I think it is something that makes our state great, but when these management practices prioritize the wealthy I think it not only has a negative impact on people who may struggle getting to the beach and our coastal ecosystems as a whole. We need to stop putting in seawalls and rip rap to mitigate erosion, that is only going to lead to sand loss and less beaches and surf breaks for everyone to enjoy. I think we need to look towards the future and know that erosion is happening and the more walls we try to put up to prevent it are only going to hurt the general public.

column221:
I believe we are not doing nearly enough to safeguard ocean and coastal resources for the future. Our focus on extracting from the ocean for industry, commercial and recreational use as well as pollution coming from land and entering the sea, noise pollution, vessel strikes, and climate change related impacts are causing cumulative harm that will only get worse. 

Even in environmentally aware communities like Santa Cruz where I live, the local elected officials are more concerned about short term concerns such as protecting real estate and promoting fishing than in making hard choices to better protect the ocean and coast as the highest priority. 

A couple of recent cases in point - a proposal to pilot making West Cliff Drive one lane to better accommodate sea level rise was discontinued after neighbors complained about traffic impacts. And a proposal to enhance marine protected area protections was similarly rejected by local elected officials without any serious consideration after recreational fishermen complained.

column221: I was born in Fort Bragg in 1955 and lived on our tribal land called Noyo. As a child, I remember my grandmother pounding abalone and frying it. We ate it with seaweed. While we still gather seaweed and mussels, we can no longer gather abalone. Due to years of over harvesting and kelp forests dwindling because of sea urchin overpopulation, my grandsons may never have the opportunity to eat the abalone that our people gathered along this coast. Although there are other reasons for the abalone decline like warming waters, we must not forget about the Russians who killed all the sea otters who controlled the sea urchins. It would be great if sea otters came back to eat the sea urchins so that the bull kelp can grow and provide food for the abalone.

In short, especially as the current government is slashing funds for ocean science and ocean protections, I recommend you continue to establish new avenues for volunteer education and participation.

Personally, I feel like Del Norte County is often forgotten by the greater state of California. We have great coastal access but zero maintenance (or funding for maintenance) for trails, trash cans, and other facilities. 

Currently, we do not have "locking" or "bear-safe" trash cans near the beach. This is an issue because of the wildlife presence (largely racoons, but also black bear). Every night, every piece of trash in those trash cans are on the ground. This is a huge issue that the county needs to address.

column221: Open ocean swimming, diving for abalone, playing beach volleyball, windsurfing and kayaking were weekly activities for me in my 20s, 30s and 40s. I established many community connections through these activities, that helped me be successful at work as an engineer and later as a public high school teacher. Today, as a kayaker and as a community volunteer with Friends of the Lost Coast and Friends of the Dunes, I am paying back only some of the value California’s coast has brought to my life. I struggle with the constraints that impact the livelihoods of my friends who fish for a living, and yet the consequences for poachers and illegal fishing is not nearly harsh enough. I am absolutely against ANY offshore drilling or mining - the disasters that have occurred in the Gulf of Mexico are still being felt by too many communities along those coasts. We CANNOT ignore the negative impacts of increasing ocean temperatures and pollution either. From the decline in sea star populations and the resulting anemone barrens, to  increasingly common leptospirosis outbreaks, we need to understand the underlying causes and take real steps towards mitigation.

column221: I appreciate MPAs that allow for low impact consumptive take. I disapprove of MPAs that bar take (e.g. SMRs). There is a deep distrust of MPAs in the fishing/spearfishing community. 30x30 marine should attempt to be more like 30x30 land in the criteria for which the program considers an acre conserved. Much of the 30x30 land acreage allows for consumptive take in the form of hunting or mushroom picking, whereas 30x30 marine acre primarily only counts MPA reserves but not national marine sanctuaries except for Chumash. 30x30 would get a lot more buy-in from the fishing community if it shifted its focus on curbing development, industrial usage, and water quality.

column221: I would like to see something done to protect our area, specifically from commercial crabbing and fishing boats. Over 95% of our seafood gets shipped to china. This industry exploits our ocean and does not benefit our community. Jobs in the fishing industry could be replaced by bringing in jobs related to marine protection and science. I recently overheard a conversation where someone was telling another crabber how a whale had been tangled in their lines and was trying to scrape the lines off itself, trying to get free. I constantly see crabbing and fishing boats and whales in the same area. How are we letting this continue? How are we allowing whales to be caught in crabbing lines? A friend of mine works on the fishing and crabbing boats, and says that although there are regulations, once the boats get out to sea many of the rules go out the window. For example, they simply throw all waste overboard. Our oceans are being harmed for an industry that exploits it and creates virtually no benefit to the local community. I personally want to see this end. If there is anything you know of that I can do, or if there is anyone I can talk to about this, please let me know. My phone number is (707)954-2822. My email address is jaiamhudson@gmail.com.
